flashcards.category: Grade 12 Biology: Vascular Plant Anatomy & Physiology

cards.front: What are the two types of **vascular tissue**?
cards.back: **Xylem** and **Phloem**.
Transports water and dissolved minerals from roots upward. Provides structural support.
Transports sugars (products of photosynthesis) from sources to sinks.
A strengthening polymer found in xylem cell walls.

cards.front: What are the two types of xylem conducting cells?
cards.back: **Tracheids** and **Vessel Elements**.

cards.front: How do tracheids and vessel elements differ?
cards.back: Tracheids: long, thin, overlapping, pits. Vessel elements: shorter, wider, have perforation plates for direct flow.

cards.front: What is the conducting cell in phloem?
cards.back: **Sieve tube elements**.
Parenchyma cells adjacent to sieve tube elements that support their function.

cards.front: What are the 3 main plant organs?
cards.back: Roots, Stems, Leaves.

cards.front: Two main types of root systems?
cards.back: **Taproot** (one main root) and **Fibrous** (many branching roots).

cards.front: What are the functions of roots?
cards.back: Anchorage, absorption of water/minerals, storage.
A protective layer covering the root tip.
At the tips of roots and shoots (site of primary growth).
Epidermal extensions increasing surface area for water absorption.
Inner layer of the root cortex with a Casparian strip that regulates water/mineral entry to vascular cylinder.
A waxy barrier in endodermal cells forcing water to pass through cell membranes.
Layer inside endodermis where lateral roots originate.
Support leaves/flowers, transport, storage.
Node: where leaf attaches. Internode: stem segment between nodes.
An undeveloped shoot containing an apical meristem.
Small openings on stems for gas exchange.
Photosynthesis.
Waxy layer on leaves preventing water loss.
Pores on leaf surface for gas exchange.
Cells that open/close stomata.

cards.front: What are the two types of **mesophyll**?
cards.back: **Palisade** (tightly packed, top) and **Spongy** (loosely packed, bottom with air spaces).
Loss of water vapor from plant leaves via stomata.
Water pulled up xylem by transpiration pull (tension), maintained by cohesion of water molecules and adhesion to xylem walls.
Attraction between water molecules (hydrogen bonding).
Attraction between water molecules and xylem walls.
Osmotic push forcing water upward in xylem, often resulting in guttation.
Exudation of water droplets from leaf margins via hydathodes.

cards.front: What is the **Pressure-Flow hypothesis** for phloem?
cards.back: At source: sugar loading → water enters → pressure increases → bulk flow to sink where sugar is unloaded.
Source: where sugar is produced/loaded (e.g., mature leaf). Sink: where sugar is used/stored (e.g., root, fruit).

cards.front: What are the 3 main plant tissue types?
cards.back: **Dermal**, **Vascular**, **Ground**.
Protection, water regulation. Epidermis, cuticle, guard cells.
Fills interior. Includes parenchyma, collenchyma, sclerenchyma.
Thin-walled, living; storage, photosynthesis, can differentiate.
Thickened, living; flexible support.
Thick lignin walls, dead at maturity; rigid support.
